data.title: I'm Buying Up To 12% Yield For Recurring Income
data.description:
Now is a great time to be an income investor, especially as the market remains volatile. This has resulted in a number of beaten down names. That's why I remain focused on businesses that generate predictable cash flows. Even better is if cash flows are backed by hard assets and/or are contracted with conservative underwriting standards.

This brings me to the following 2 picks, both of which are trading in bargain territory, with yields ranging from 6% to 12%. This could make plenty of sense for investors who wish to generate cash flow while getting a solid margin of safety, which are keys to sleeping well at night. Let's get started on what makes each a compelling high-yielding 'Buy' at present!

#1: Morgan Stanley Direct Lending - 12% Yield

Morgan Stanley Direct Lending (MSDL) is an externally managed BDC that focuses on first-lien debt investments to middle market companies. It has a fairly defensive portfolio, with just 20% exposure to Software. As shown below, its Insurance Services, IT Services, and Commercial Services are its next largest segments.

MSDL is also diversified by borrowers, with no position representing over 2% of portfolio total. The borrower base is comprised of 227 portfolio companies with an average position size of just 40 bps. Moreover, MSDL's portfolio is mostly comprised of first lien debt investments, representing 96% of total investments.

MSDL benefits from its association with its 'big brother' Morgan Stanley (MS). This is reflected by 76% of its deals having multiple MS touchpoints. As shown below, MSIM has $1.9 trillion of total AUM and has a 40-year track record of alternative assets management.

MSDL has a portfolio size of $3.8 billion and generated NII per share of $0.49 during Q4 2025. This was down by $0.01 sequentially and down by $0.07 YoY due to lower interest rates. NAV per share declined by a modest $0.15 sequentially, due primarily to the restructuring and sale of one of its portfolio holdings. Nearly 70% of non-refinancing activity during Q4 was driven by new platforms, demonstrating healthy sponsor engagement.

MSDL's NAV per share has ebbed and flowed over the past 5 years within the $19.80 to $20.80 range, as shown below. Non-accruals remain low at just 1.6% of portfolio cost. Plus, PIK (payment in kind) income, represents just 3.9% of total investment income.

The market seems to be overly rotated on MSDL's software exposure, given the share price performance in recent weeks. This is considering the fact that MSDL's software exposure is modest at 20%. It also has strong protections with an average loan-to-value ratio of 40%, implying 60% equity buffer. Plus, borrower interest coverage ratios actually improved over the past year.

Moreover, MSDL is seeing a rebound in deal activity, as reflected by heighted private equity deal momentum in the second half of last year. This is supported by a sound balance sheet with a debt-to-equity ratio of 1.2x, sitting well below the 2.0 statutory limit for BDCs.

Importantly, the current reset dividend rate of $0.45 is well covered by NII/share of $0.49. MSDL also trades at a substantial discount to its NAV at the current price of $14.90. This equates to a P/NAV ratio of 0.74x, sitting near its all time low since inception, as shown below.

At the current price, I believe the market is way too pessimistic around the stock. This is considering its safe loan-to-value ratio, modest software exposure and well-covered dividend. As such, MSDL could deliver compelling total return based on the 12% dividend yield and material discount from NAV.

#2: Four Corners Property Trust - 6% Yield

Four Corners Property Trust (FCPT) is a net lease REIT that focuses on restaurant and service-oriented retail properties across the U.S. It leases to creditworthy tenants under long-term triple-net leases in which the tenant is responsible for paying property taxes, maintenance, and insurance.

At present, FCPT has 1,325 leases that are spread across 179 brands. Its portfolio is 99.6% occupied with strong tenant EBITDAR-to-rent coverage of 5.1x. Plus, 53% of FCPT's ABR (annual base rent) comes from investment grade rated tenants.

Since being spun-off from Darden Restaurants (DRI), FCPT has greatly diversified its portfolio. As shown below, legacy DRI assets leased to Olive Garden and Longhorn Steakhouse now fall well under 50% of portfolio rent.

FCPT demonstrated steady performance during Q4 2025, with AFFO per share rising by 2.2% and 2.9% YoY for the quarter and the full year, respectively. This was driven by annual rent escalators and portfolio growth. FCPT's tenants are also doing well, with Chili's reporting 9% same-store sales growth, and Olive Garden and Longhorn seeing 5% and 6% YoY sales growth, respectively.

Last year, FCPT also acquired 105 properties for $318 million at an attractive 6.8% blended cap rate. Most acquisitions were one-off properties in the $5 to $20 million range. This lessens the risk of overpaying for large portfolio transactions.

Looking ahead, FCPT has continued opportunities to diversify its portfolio, as management is looking into grocery stores, equipment rental properties, automotive services, and medical retail. These are segments that are recession and e-commerce resilient and tend to perform well even during economic downturns.

FCPT is supported by a solid balance sheet with Baa3 and BBB investment grade credit ratings from Moody's and Fitch. It has a safe net debt to EBITDA ratio of 4.9x, sitting below target range of 5x to 6x, and has a strong fixed charge coverage ratio 4.8x. FCPT currently yields 5.7% and the dividend is well-covered by an 82% payout ratio.

FCPT is in value range at the current price of $25.54 with a forward P/FFO of 14.3, sitting far below its historical P/FFO of 17.3, as shown below.

At the current price, FCPT would only need to deliver around 4% annual FFO/share growth in combination with the near-6% dividend yield. This forms a base case for potential 10% annual total returns, which I believe is reasonably achievable. Plus, any reversion to the mean valuation could result in market beating total returns.

Risks to Consider

Risks to MSDL include lower interest rates, which could pressure its floating rate investment yields. Unforeseen credit risks could also pose an issue, and could result in a higher non-accrual rate. Increased competition could also pressure investment yields.

Risks to FCPT include its portfolio concentration on restaurant properties. Tightening household budgets could result in less dining out by consumers. Higher interest rate could raise FCPT cost of debt and interest expense. Plus, new investment categories may be out of management's core field of expertise.

Investor Takeaway

Both MSDL and FCPT stand out as attractive high-yield opportunities for income investors seeking dependable cash flow in a volatile market environment. Each company generates stable income backed by hard assets and contracts.

MSDL offers exposure to a diversified portfolio of mostly first-lien loans to middle-market companies. It's supported by conservative underwriting, low non-accrual levels, and a strong relationship with Morgan Stanley's broader platform. It currently trades at a steep discount to its NAV with a 12% yield.

Meanwhile, FCPT delivers steady real estate income through long-term triple-net leases to well-known restaurant and service tenants. It maintains high occupancy, strong tenant rent coverage, and an investment-grade balance sheet. FCPT offers a 5.7% yield at a valuation below its historical average.

Together, these two names provide investors with a high income stream while providing a margin of safety due to their low valuations. For these reasons, they make solid 'Buys' for a diversified income portfolio.

data.title: AMD: Pullback After Blockbuster Deal Sets Up An Amazing Buy Point
data.description:
AMD: Game-Changing Partnership With Meta

Is it finally time for investors in Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) to finally take the shackles off that have humbled and kept the semiconductor leader from benefiting from a higher valuation upgrade and keeping pace with Nvidia (NVDA)? While Nvidia is a $4.37 trillion behemoth, AMD’s market cap of just over $311 billion pales in (relative) comparison. No surprises then, that for AMD to demonstrate that it is capable of becoming a dominant standard in its own way against its much bigger, more illustrious, and arguably more successful “bigger brother." Then, we should expect the company to provide more incentives, so to speak, in convincing bigger companies, customers, and partners to give them a chance to demonstrate that Lisa Su means business, but they still need a platform, allowing them to scale and prove that they have the capability to not just keep Nvidia on its edge, but also become more influential and present in customer negotiations.

Is the AI infrastructure buildout dead? Nonsense. Far from it, we are not even close to the peak on the multi-year narrative that is expected to take us through to 2030. And please keep in mind that AMD has given us its longer-term prognosis, right? I don't think I need to point out to you the financial day numbers; you can find them for yourself. Nevertheless, a quick reminder: the outlook is for 35% revenue CAGR through the rest of the decade, and achieving a medium-term $20 EPS as well.

So it is definitely aggressive, but we expect nothing less from Lisa Su, right? Much has been said about whether AMD possesses the right technological stack, because Nvidia's CUDA ecosystem is going to become even stronger, right? Jensen Huang builds Nvidia on top of CUDA, whether it's for physical AI, AI factories, or even data centers across the hyperscaler or enterprise space. And for those of us who understand the immense power of network effects, I don't think I need to remind you how formidable this moat is, because when most developers are using your ecosystem, they aggregate even more value behind it, and underpinning the platform not only increases its dominance, but also helps keep your archrivals away from taking share away from you. In a recent commentary, Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang brought us through his train of thought on how these line up for Nvidia:

It's a 5-layer cake, right, exactly. But because we've been working on it so long, it looks like everything is accelerated. But it's not true. It's because I did it one at a time, one domain at a time that all of the important domains in the world are now fully accelerated. And so the thing that we do on the supply chain side, our balance sheet is incredibly valuable because it provides security for our customers. On the upstream side, I'm cultivating new ecosystems for the future. All these AI natives that I'm investing in, the companies that we're partnering with, these are expanding, extending the CUDA ecosystem. 100% of everything that we do is on top of CUDA. Every investment that we've made is on top of CUDA. - Nvidia presents at Morgan Stanley Technology, Media & Telecom Conference 2026

Before we carry on, if you do not understand what this Nvidia five-layer cake is, Jensen Huang enunciated at the recent conference in Davos, as he emphasized why the company has become so entrenched across the technology sphere, as he shared how “AI [is not] a single technology but [is] a "five-layer cake," spanning energy, chips and computing infrastructure, cloud data centers, AI models, and, ultimately, the application layer.”

And I wanted to bring you through this preamble for us to better understand and appreciate just how challenging it has been for AMD in trying to convince the market, its customers, and its technological partners that it has what it takes to provide them more than a viable option other than being stuck in and locked into the CUDA ecosystem.

However, as we have learned from the AMD-OpenAI deal late last year, it also entailed AMD issuing them performance-based warrants that considered the installation capacity over time, and it seems like AMD has also gone to a similar playbook in executing the recent deal with Meta Platforms (META). So, I'm not that surprised that AMD clinched the deal while offering the warrants to Meta to strengthen their technological partnership.

AMD needed this deal. The market was already getting skeptical of OpenAI's narrative. Even though I upgraded AMD stock back to a Buy in my previous update, the fact that it was struggling for traction underscores just how uncertain the market has turned recently, since it doesn't have quite the level of market share and concentration as compared to Nvidia. So any kind of marquee arrangements that could rival the scale that the company nailed with OpenAI previously are going to make everyone more excited once more with AMD’s proposition.

In addition, these partnerships are not exactly new, right? While they may exist in a different form, Nvidia has also engaged in such strengthening propositions with their suppliers, their partners, and their customers. We also know that Nvidia provides data center lease guarantees and most recently participated in the $30 billion funding round to support a massive valuation of $840 billion for OpenAI, but the fact of the matter is, AMD is not going to print $200 billion in free cash flow anytime soon. Nvidia’s FCF projections are expected to almost hit the $230 billion mark by the end of FY2027, while AMD is expected to deliver just shy of the $7.4 billion mark by the end of this fiscal year. May I impress upon you the substantive disconnect?

So, I believe AMD is leveraging what it could deliver, while banking on the market's penchant for its higher potential for growth, as it attempts to gain more market share in merchant AI chips over that of Nvidia. But of course, unless your customers are truly convinced of your standards and your performance, then why should they switch, right? And if not, they will need something to incentivize their relationship to a higher level, in my opinion.

AMD Can Take Share With These Deals

The Magnificent Seven, arguably the main customers for all the biggest AI infrastructure spending through the rest of the decade, will see their operating cash flow numbers fall further this year. And if you consider next year, they need to spend more money to build out and compete even more aggressively against the rivals, which will necessitate them to optimize not just in terms of spending, but also the technology allowing them to acquire the best performance for watt per dollar.

However, if the technology has not been extensively tested, deployed at scale, or even convinced independent reviewers that they are better (because semianalysis also said AMD still has more work to do to close the gap with Nvidia in inference in a recent technical paper), then, it's hard to imagine how any disciplined, credible management of the Mag 7 will be more than willing to invest their balance sheet so aggressively without a sweetener that could move the needle, or shift the sentiments profoundly. Yes, I believe this is a sweetheart deal that AMD offered Meta, and I think I am not the only one who thought that way, as BofA analyst Vivek Arya opined to management when they announced that deal recently:

Lisa (AMD CEO), I had a more fundamental question, which is where is the economic value add here? You're giving away $30 billion roughly of value in your stock. And in return, you're getting about $30 billion, $35 billion of net income. So it seems like an even swap. So where is the value add here? So if I ask it in a different way, if the product is so good, why does AMD need to give up 10% of your equity? And is now every MI450 customer going to ask for this kind of a deal? - AMD Discusses Expanded AI Partnership with Meta and Multi-Year GPU Deployment Agreement

I thought that was an astute question from a sharp and experienced analyst, who spotted the economic value conundrum. Lisa Su’s reply mainly hovers around the “transformative” nature of the opportunity. I mean, I could understand that narrative. Because it means Meta could be building up to six gigawatts of capacity with AMD chips over several years, with one gigawatt expected to be deployed in 2026. So that's a good start, but of course the warrants vesting will also happen with the first GW deployed.

However, the market appears to be okay because the stock is still way up by 100% in the past one year, despite these pretty valid questions around the economic value of whether it truly benefits AMD or not. And if it is so good, why does AMD need to give away equity?

Well, I believe the reason for the market indicates that AMD could turn out to be a faster grower in the next few years. Based on Nvidia’s most recent earnings scorecard, I think it's fair to say that Jensen Huang delivered an outstanding result. Yet, I think the market is not looking at just CY2026. They are looking at whether AMD can sustain that multi-year 35% CAGR, and that will likely be the main difference that separates whether investors will continue to accord a premium to AMD shares over that of NVDA.

I'm of the opinion the market thinks that the potential for capital appreciation through more opportunities for share gains should offset these dilution worries. And since the vesting schedule will be in progressive phases, very similar to the one that they inked for OpenAI, the market hasn't been surprised to the downside. I think management did a good job in keeping the deal terms as close as possible, including the total gigawatt capacity.

Therefore, unless the execution falters unexpectedly, which could really happen, right? Supply chain factors going down to capacity, HBM, and advanced packaging, all these matters and constraints could feature more prominently, even as Nvidia reminds us that they have gotten it all sorted out, while AMD has indicated that they are not seeing any significant stress factors that could hold them back.

To lend further support, AMD also indicated that its CPU business is going through a much-welcomed revival that Lisa Su pointed out in her remarks. The market was possibly baking in a technological transition from CPU to GPU that could benefit Nvidia profusely at the expense of AMD. But it seems like such a one-way transition hasn't quite panned out (because we still need data center CPUs more than you think, yeah?). And, I think there is little hesitation that Intel (INTC) and AMD are the behemoths in this business, and this is something the market, I believe, will give the benefit of the doubt to its proven execution over the years, as AI inference becomes even more crucial as agentic AI proliferates across the enterprise:

And then frankly, we see just a tremendous demand for traditional compute as well. So if you look at the CPU cycle, we've always believed that the computing stack is heterogeneous and you're going to need CPUs and GPUs and FPGAs and all of these components. And that's really coming to fruition here in 2026. So a few months ago, we had our Financial Analyst Day. We put out an ambitious financial model to grow sort of 35% CAGR over sort of the next 3-, 4-, 5-year period. And I think as we look at the market dynamics, as we look at the product dynamics, I think we are very much on track to that and with an ambitious target of over $20 of earnings per share in that time frame. So lots to be excited about. - AMD Presents at Morgan Stanley Technology, Media & Telecom Conference 2026 Transcript

That's right. Pulling the plug on the data center CPU doesn't make sense, right? Even Intel is enjoying a mini revival on their own, breaking above the $50 level earlier this year, although it has subsided somewhat, in line with the recent weakness seen in the technology sector and semiconductor plays.

Buy Or Sell AMD Stock?

So if you ask me, this AMD outperformance has been a long time in the making, after suffering underperformance against Nvidia for several years, as Jensen Huang takes center stage. The reversal against Nvidia came early last year. And if you contemplate how the trend has been going up (follow my upward-pointing arrow annotated in the above chart), I think it's easy to understand why the market is banking on the faster-growing company through the rest of the decade.

AMD’s forward earnings multiple of 30x is valued above the 22x that marks Nvidia’s multiple right now. So sure, even if you were to say that the market is pricing in a premium, I agree with you. But so what? If the company can expect to gain more share and have better visibility to achieve its analyst day targets through this momentous partnership with the largest hyperscalers, big tech companies, and the AI pure plays, why shouldn't the market be more excited?

And look at the price action; when overlaying AMD over NVDA, the trend bias is consistently pointing to the upside. So even if we were to buy more AMD, we are following the dominant trend, so the risk-reward is strongly in our favor right now, especially since the market action is now retesting the key support levels as I indicated above along the blue line (50-week moving average)

Rating: Maintain Buy.

data.title: Grocery Outlet: No Turnaround In Sight
data.description:
Grocery Outlet Holding Corp. (GO) reported another weak quarter, sending the stock plummeting after earnings. The value grocer’s Q4 showed sales growth slightly below the company’s already weak guidance, and the 2026 outlook doesn’t suggest any improvement in upcoming quarters. Such weakness is pressuring earnings, and has led Grocery Outlet to close a number of underperforming locations. Grocery Outlet’s value perception is deteriorating.

I maintained a Hold rating in my previous November 2025 article on the stock, titled “Grocery Outlet: In Need Of A Major Refresh.” The stock has since lost a further -49% of its value, meanwhile the S&P 500 (SP500) has returned 1%. The stock’s valuation now clearly reflects weak expectations.

Grocery Outlet Q4 Review

There weren’t any meaningful signals of a turnaround in Grocery Outlet’s Q4 report. I believe that the core focus remains on the company’s sales performance, which was increasingly concerning. Revenues came in at $1.22 billion, growing by 10.7% year-on-year due to an additional week in the fiscal quarter as well as Grocery Outlet’s continued store openings. Signaling the store performance underneath, Grocery Outlet’s comparable store sales declined by -0.8% due to a lower average transaction size, leading Grocery Outlet to slightly miss its 2025 guidance on comparable sales. Comparable store sales have continued to decelerate.

The topline result missed Wall Street’s already weak consensus expectation by $13 million, and the adjusted EPS also missed expectations slightly as a result.

For comparison, U.S. food inflation currently stands at 2.1%, in my opinion working as a benchmark for an acceptable comparable store sales growth rate. The Kroger Co. (KR) latest reported 2.4% comparable growth, Costco Wholesale Corporation (COST) showed 5.8% U.S. comparable growth for January, and Albertsons Companies, Inc.’s (ACI) latest 2.4% growth in the metric has even caused market concerns.

A value grocer like Grocery Outlet should typically thrive in an economic setting where consumer confidence is low; the company was even clearly countercyclical during the 2008-2009 financial crisis. Instead, the Grocery Outlet brand has now clearly lost its value perception. The company also notes that competitors have increased promotional activity, further pressuring Grocery Outlet’s value proposition.

There are no clear improvements guided for 2026 as comparable sales only decelerated in January. Grocery Outlet expects total revenues of $4.60-4.72 billion for the year, reflecting very weak -2% to 0% comparable store sales growth. At the mid-point, comparable store sales are only decelerating further in upcoming quarters.

Earnings did improve in Q4, but only temporarily. Better inventory management more than offset pressure from increased promotional activity and markdowns, leading to a 20 basis point gross margin gain. Adjusted EBITDA came in at $68.0 million, growing by 18.8% year-on-year.

The improvement was achieved mostly due to one extra week in the fiscal quarter, though. Adjusted EBITDA is guided at $220-235 million for 2026, down by $26.8 million from 2025 at the mid-point. Fixed cost deleverage from lower comparable sales, and increased promotions, weigh significantly on the earnings outlook. The gross margin is guided at 29.7-30.0%, down from 30.3% in 2025. An earnings turnaround isn’t in sight.

The report leaves Grocery Outlet in a very uncertain position. Such an underperformance has pushed the company to decide to close 36 of the company’s 570 stores and a related distribution center during 2026, more than offsetting targeted 30-33 store openings for the year. Grocery Outlet clearly needs to reestablish its brand image as a value leader through improved merchandising, but regaining the value perception after losing it has so far turned out to be challenging. Investments in store refreshes also need to improve the brand’s perception. Continued weakness makes Grocery Outlet’s store opening plan increasingly questionable.

Without major reported improvements in the comparable store sales performance, I believe that investors shouldn’t expect much going forward.

Grocery Outlet’s Valuation Reflects Weak Expectations

I updated my discounted cash flow (DCF) model for Grocery Outlet. I still estimate slightly elevated growth in the 2027-2029 period, reflecting Grocery Outlet’s store opening plan but underperforming comparable store sales underneath. I ultimately still estimate Grocery Outlet’s comparable store sales to stabilize at 1.5% over the long term while store openings halt.

I now estimate profitability using adjusted EBITDA. I expect the EBITDA margin to show a further decline to 4%, reflecting profitability pressure from lower comparable sales and higher promotions in upcoming years as well. The estimate doesn’t expect a rebound in Grocery Outlet’s performance.

In the cash flow conversion, I estimate half of EBITDA to be taxed at a 25% tax rate, that Grocery Outlet’s CapEx will gradually slow down from $170 million in 2026 to $100 million in CapEx by 2030, and that working capital growth and miscellaneous cash expenses cause a $20 million annual headwind on free cash flow.

The estimates put Grocery Outlet’s fair value at $7.4, suggesting 12% upside as of writing. Grocery Outlet’s valuation now clearly reflects depressed earnings expectations, mostly for good reason as the performance has continued to weaken. A turnaround could potentially create massive upside potential, but without improvements in sight, I believe that it’s too early to price in meaningful progress.

The fair value estimate is down from $13.9 previously.

CAPM

The DCF model uses a cost of capital [WACC] of 6.46%, derived from a capital asset pricing model:

I estimate a 6.26% interest rate, annualized from Q4 debt and interest expenses. I now assume a slightly higher 40% long-term debt-to-equity ratio as Grocery Outlet’s equity value has declined.

For cost of equity, I use the 10-year bond yield of 4.11% as the risk-free rate. The equity risk premium of 4.18% is Professor Aswath Damodaran’s most recent January estimate for the U.S. I have kept the beta estimate at 0.61. With a liquidity premium of 0.5%, the cost of equity stands at 7.16% and the WACC at 6.46%.

Takeaway

There isn’t a turnaround in sight yet. Grocery Outlet’s sales performance is only deepening despite increased promotions. The brand’s value perception has deteriorated. Store closures during 2026, and a concerning financial outlook for the year, weaken hopes of a recovery. As weak earnings are already priced in, and an unlikely turnaround could now create massive upside, I remain at a Hold rating for GO stock.

data.title: Iran Conflict: A Reality Check On The Surprise Move Of Drone Stocks
data.description:
The conflict in Iran potentially has an incremental positive impact for the aerospace and defense sectors industry as a whole. However, one area where I believe the region has found it lacks some strength is missile and counter-drone defense. In this report, I discuss several names that saw a significant move in the stock price and how they could benefit from increased demand for counter-drone solutions. The analysis focuses on what each company provides in terms of neutralizing drone-class deployment by Iran. My findings show that the most capable C-UAS providers have not seen the strongest share price appreciation. Some of the companies in this report are already part of our coverage and have price targets set using the evoX Stock Screener developed by The Aerospace Forum. When that is the case, the price targets have been added.

Iran Strikes Back With Missile and Drones

While the U.S. and Israel have hit targets in Iran, Tehran has responded by attacking targets in neighboring countries. In some instances, US bases were attacked, but there are also instances where infrastructure, including airports and energy infrastructure, has been hit. In my view, it has painfully exposed that Iran’s neighboring countries are not well protected against what I consider to be a relatively contained response from Iran. Whether intensity increases in the days and weeks ahead with higher frequency or more impactful weapon systems remains to be seen, but I would say that the first few days should have rung an alarm bell for neighboring countries regarding their ability to defend themselves against drone attacks. Drones are relatively cheap to make, and what we saw with the drone crisis in Europe is that many countries still lack cost efficient counter-drone solutions.

The Companies In Focus For Counter-Drone or C-UAS

In a broad sense there are many companies that have exposure to counter-drone technology. That's because there also is indirect involvement of some companies for counter-drone solutions. Kratos (KTOS) for instance, provides microwave electronics and subsystems that can be used in radar and jamming activities and target drones for training purposes. So, Kratos has exposure through the detection layer and the jamming layer as well as training. L3Harris Technologies (LHX) has signal jamming devices, while other big contractors such as RTX (RTX), Lockheed Martin (LMT) and Saab (SAABF) produce radars, interceptors, and drone-to-kill-drones solutions. These companies have exposure to counter-drone solutions demand, but they are less effective at capitalizing on C-UAS demand.

The companies with more direct C-UAS exposure can be divided into two groups. The first one is pure C-UAS, and the second is Drone+C-UAS.

C-UAS Companies:

  1. DroneShield (DRSHF)
  2. Electro Optic Systems (EOPSF)
  3. Ondas (ONDS)

Drone+C-UAS:

  • AeroVironment (AVAV)
  • Red Cat (RCAT)
  • Draganfly (DPRO)

DroneShield Potentially Is Not The Best Positioned

DroneShield stock rose 5% following the attacks in Iran and the country’s response. However, I believe that DroneShield potentially is one of the worst-positioned companies to benefit from increased C-UAS demand for the type of drone deployment seen in Iran. There's a huge global opportunity, but if we look at what DroneShield offers, we note that the company provides counter-UAS through a drone detection sensor system plus a soft-kill method. Soft-kill means that signal jamming is utilized to bring drones down. However, the drones that Iran uses are hard to jam. They're kamikaze drones, often with pre-programmed paths and internal inertial navigation units. So, even if a drone is being jammed, it can still rely on its internal components to potentially reach a target. So, the main opportunity really is the drone traction layer that can inform hard-kill methods. That means that I do not consider DroneShield the best solution, as they lack a hard-kill method, but they have opportunities in the detection layer market.

For DroneShield, I have a $3.51 price target, implying 42% upside. This is driven by an acceleration in sales in 2025, improving operating leverage in as commercialization accelerates, and that also drives the free cash flow expectations, with free cash flow accelerating from 2027 onwards.

Electro Optic System May Be Positioned In A Superior Way Among Foreign Providers

One company that may be positioned best of all is Electro Optic Systems. The company has pivoted towards a C-UAS in recent years. It does have a remote weapon system, which is simply a mountable cannon. Combining this 30mm cannon with a radar provides their kinetic kill solution. The more promising aspect, especially for addressing drone swarms in a cost-effective way, is a non-kinetic solution using high-energy lasers, or HELs. This is what the company provides with its in-development Apollo HEL. The company also provides the TITANIS integrated counter-drone defense layer for detection and tracking of drones. Using this system, either the kinetic-kill or non-kinetic-kill method can be selected. So, EOS provides solutions from detection to hard kill methods with non-kinetic and kinetic options. The stock price has risen almost 10% since the attacks on Iran started.

For EOS, I have a $7.54 price target, implying around 8% upside from current prices based on an EV/sales assessment. The company does not have a reflective and positive EBITDA trajectory that allows for a meaningful price target determination. That's why I have used an EV/sales assessment instead. The key metrics show a significant improvement for 2026 as the company has pivoted toward being a more drone- and counter-drone-centered company with growth prospects that will likely accelerate beyond the current forecast window.

Ondas Iron Drone Raider and RF Solutions

Ondas stock initially rose 20% after the markets opened, but the stock price is now more or less where it was before the Iran war started. The reason is that Ondas' product line is, in my view, highly ineffective at catching loitering munitions like the Shahed drones. The way Ondas’ hard-kill Iron Drone Raider works is that it can be integrated with a third-party detection radar. If a radar detects an incoming drone, the Iron Drone Raider flies to the drone and shoots a net at the drone. For high-speed drones like the Shahed-136, this is not an effective method, and it works only against small drones. So, Ondas' hard-kill solution is made for hobby drones getting too close to critical infrastructure, but it's not at all equipped to high-speed drones like the Shahead, which weighs around 200 kg.

The company also has a soft-kill capability, but it uses radiofrequency detection and jamming to redirect drones. For pre-programmed drones, the RF detection and redirection method does not work.

Ondas scores a C+ in Seeking Alpha's Quant, driven by high EV/EBITDA, EV/sales, and price-to-book valuations compared to peers. The company's growth is scored a B as revenue growth is expected to accelerate; that would still indicate sales-driven valuation multiples that are substantially higher than the peer group.

AeroVironment May Have the Most Attractive US C-UAS Line Up

AeroVironment has significantly strengthened its C-UAS solutions through the acquisition of BlueHalo. AeroVironment is extremely familiar with loitering munitions drones, so the combination with BlueHalo makes for a very strong expertise driven solution for drones and C-UAS. Detection and tracking methods include radiofrequency as well as infrared and optical solutions and optional radar integration with kill solutions ranging from soft-kill to hard-kill with kinetic kill solutions in the form of the Freedom Eagle 1 missile and the LOCUST system, which is a non-kinetic laser weapon system. Just like EOS, AeroVironment has a laser weapon system. There are, however, two differences. The EOS laser weapon system is still in development, while LOCUST is already deployed. The EOS HEL is not yet widely deployed, contrary to LOCUST which is in operational testing. Additionally, the EOS system is a higher power class system.

Ironically, AeroVironment has one of the most capable drone and counter-drone solutions, but the stock first rose 20% before falling nearly 33% from that high, driven by a downgrade from Raymond James due to SCAR contract continuity concerns. Since then, AeroVironment did outline that the SCAR contract is on hold as the contract is being reformed to a fixed-price contract. The stock is now down 10% from the pre-war closing price.

For AeroVironment, I have a $324.27 price target against the expected earnings for 2028. This implies 43% upside from the current prices driven by expectations that C-UAS and drone demand will continue to see robust growth with a stronger cost amortization base following the acquisition of BlueHalo.

Red Cat Is More of a C-UAS Kitty

Red Cat (RCAT) stock has rallied 26%. Looking at their counter-drone solutions, that surge is not understandable at all. Why? Simply because Red Cat has no strong C-UAS product. Red Cat provides reconnaissance drones. Does it make the company less attractive? Not necessarily. For mobile dynamic monitoring, Red Cat essentially provides the DJI drones of the battlefield, which can be important to monitor strategic infrastructure assets. Additionally, the company has a FANG, which is a first-person strike drone. This drone carries explosive charges that can be used to neutralize drones, although that is not the main purpose. The drone could be used to kill Shahed drones, but it's not a scalable solution, with the Shahed drone also having a velocity advantage. So, it's better scalable in terms of costs compared to missiles but lacks efficiency against laser weapons.

Red Cat scores a C+ in Seeking Alpha's Quant, driven by high EV/EBITDA, EV/sales, and price-to-book valuations compared to peers. The company's growth is scored an A- as revenue growth is expected to accelerate, but that would still indicate sales-driven valuation multiples that are substantially higher than the peer group.

Draganfly Is Focused On Intelligence, Surveillance and Reconnaissance

Draganfly (DPRO) share prices increased 4.9% after surging as much as 16%. I would say it's a interesting move for the stock given that they're not engaged in counter-UAS development, but just like Red Cat they have reconnaissance drones.

For Draganfly, I actually have a sell rating. However, we note that the stock price has substantially increased, valuing the stock in line with drone makers such as AeroVironment. While I'm not convinced that this valuation is justified given the difference in the drone classes for Draganfly compared to AeroVironment. Adopting this as the valuation standard would imply 46% with a $9.87 price target. A more conservative approach would indicate a $7.96 target with 18% upside from current valuation levels.

The Market May Be Wrong On This

Typically, I do not like to argue against the markets since you rarely come out of that as the winner. However, an informed look at the stock price development of several drone and counter-drone stocks shows that the most capable company was not awarded.

Assessing the drone threat from Iran, we notice that they use high-speed kamikaze drones that are hard if not impossible to redirect using a soft-kill method since the drones can rely on internal inertial navigation and pre-programmed targeting. So, there's no radiofrequency link to disrupt between the controller and the drone.

Hard-kill methods are better suited, and especially against high-speed drones, having high-energy lasers (non-kinetic) is preferred. So, we see that only EOS and AeroVironment provide or develop that, with AeroVironment having the edge with early testing deployment. The AeroVironment system has a lower power class, but that's not necessarily a limitation. It may take seconds to take out a drone. In case, drone swarms need to be targeted, a higher power class would be preferred to take out multiple drones in a shorter time window. However, AeroVironment is focusing on mobile solutions where the laser power is limited since a higher-power laser requires more cooling and thus a bigger system. What EOS is developing is more focused on a fixed solution that can be used to be installed at, for instance, airports. So, one is not necessarily better than the other, since the focus is slightly different with a trade off between power and mobility. AeroVironment is one step ahead in the development phase, with the LOCUST system already having been used by the US border patrol.

We also note that for detection and tracking of high-speed drones, having an early-warning system is needed, and radars are best suited for that. Again, that's only provided by EOS and AeroVironment in combination with hard kill methods. So, these two companies, in my view, are best positioned for detection, tracking, and kill solutions. DroneShield would have the opportunity as a detection and tracking layer to inform soft-kill or hard-kill deployment. Red Cat and Draganfly provide basically ISR and partial drone-to-kill-drone solutions. However, the ISR capability is limited, as detection needs to happen timely, and these ISR drones simply are not fully integrated into broader C-UAS systems. Red Cat is working on that, but it still does not have the most scalable solution.

Conclusion: AeroVironment Is The Underappreciated C-UAS Technology Provider

Looking at the stock price performance, the emerged threat and the stock price performance, I can only conclude that due to the contract scare, AeroVironment has remained underappreciated by investors. EOS has an in-development laser weapon system that puts it "up there" to benefit from the type of threat that Iran’s neighboring countries are not protected against well enough. DroneShield is more of a detection layer company with a soft-kill method that's not scalable nor effective against Shahed drones. The other two other two companies, Red Cat and Draganfly, have no or limited C-UAS capability, while Ondas’ solution is simply sending a drone up to shoot a net at another drone.

I strongly believe that a general misunderstanding of capability requirements has led to stock price performance and capabilities being divergent.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Micron's AI Supercycle Accelerates
data.description:
Investment Thesis

At the core of the AI memory bottleneck is Micron Technology (MU). Since my last coverage when I called the supercyle starting point, MU had a strong bull run rising by more than 45%. Their HBM3E and their next-gen HBM4 products are shipping into high-volume production with their improved speed and power efficiency, which is exactly what hyperscalers are demanding.

With 2026 High Bandwidth Memory (HBM) supply sold out and contracts locked in for multi-year supply agreements, Micron Technology is in a great position to drive their margins significantly higher as their premium mix of memory products could increase their margins by an astonishing 99% in FY26.

HBM4 Ramp Drives 99% EBITDA Growth Into FY26

I see that Micron has a commanding market lead based on the intersection of high-volume production of advanced memory products and an aggregate demand status that exceeds global supply. The main part of this financial setup is the HBM product line. I am mainly pointing to HBM3E and the newer HBM4 generations. Micron has commenced high-volume manufacturing of HBM4 with shipments ramping in Q1-26. This timeline is an acceleration occurring one quarter earlier than Micron management earlier guided. These products may directly lead to financial leverage. The HBM4 product achieves speeds of over 11 gigabits per second.

Furthermore, the HBM3E product achieved 30% less power consumption than that of the competitors' products. This efficiency of the product is critical for data centers that are focusing on managing energy costs associated with AI workloads. Besides HBM, Micron has also come up with a new product, Low Power DRAM (LPDRAM), which has been applied in server configurations. This type of product has 60% lower power consumption compared to conventional DDR memory. This has encouraged data center architects to adopt Micron's products.

I believe this product lead is in line with a supercycle led by AI demand. The expansion of AI models that need longer context windows needs higher performance memory and storage. As per Micron’s management, server demand growth accelerated from single digits to high-teens percentages. As a result, the data center solid-state drive (SSD) business hit a $1 billion run rate. Strong demand across these high-value categories leads to margin expansion.

For that, Micron guided gross margins to 68%. This margin reflects a favorable pricing environment coming from tight supply conditions. I think this tightness may continue beyond 2026. As prices increase at these elevated margin levels, the impact on profitability becomes lesser on a percentage basis but leads heavily to absolute earnings. Micron’s TTM Gross Profit Margin is at 45.31% and it has an EBIT Margin of 32.55% that is 396.33% above the sector median. I think this is pointing out that Micron Technology captures value sharply from its topline.

Moreover, the forward metrics hold this positive momentum. Revenue growth forward is 57.25% that is 461% higher than the sector median with forward EBITDA growth is projected at 99.38%. These levels indicate that the market expects the pricing environment and product mix to result in large expansion in financials. The conversion of topline to bottomline performance is visible in the forward EPS diluted growth of 225%.

Micron has secured multiyear agreements with clients. These arrangements include specific volume commitments. These are different from historical patterns where negotiations occurred only one quarter in advance. As per the management, clients now want supply assurance for three to five years. This shift supports Micron with large visibility into forward revenue and pricing. This is reducing the volatility linked with memory cycles. The entire 2026 HBM supply is already sold out, thus, this backlog in my view indicates that high-margin production lines will run at maximum utilization. This will protect the gross margin profile.

Finally, I think the integration of storage into the AI architecture plays a big role, moving forward. As processors face higher loads, AI systems need tiered memory and storage. Data moves from hot tiers (HBM) to warm tiers (LPDRAM) and cold tiers (storage drives). Micron has a solid lead in the data center SSD market with products ranging from performance TLC drives to capacity QLC drives. The 232-layer tech and in-house controller capabilities allow Micron to capture value across this entire hierarchy.

Taken together, the acceleration of HBM4 production, the sold-out status of Micron’s 2026 HBM inventory, and the superior power efficiency of the company’s memory products give Micron significant pricing power. This pricing authority can continue to amplify gross margins and drive forward earnings growth rates that vastly exceed sector medians.

All Eyes on Micron’s Upcoming Q2 Earnings

The company has announced that it will release its fiscal Q2 2026 results on March 18, 2026 after market close. Micron is currently estimated to earn an EPS of $8.58 on revenue of $19.07 billion implying a 450% YoY earnings growth as the memory cycle continues to pick up with better pricing trends.

Micron's earnings growth story is also likely to be closely correlated with strong data center demand, especially for HBM products that can run alongside AI accelerators and GPUs. The company's management commentary on HBM3E shipment volumes and the ramp-up of next-generation HBM4 products will be closely watched by the market.

Micron Revenue Capped Until 2027 as 50% Demand Goes Unfilled

The main risk impacting Micron’s financials on a forward basis is a limitation on supply output with the high capital intensity required to expand production. Despite the demand for a supercycle described by management, Micron cannot fulfill the entirety of client orders.

As per Micron’s management, their key customers are only receiving 50% to 2/3rds of their demanded volume which is creating an opportunity for their competitors to gain share if they can identify the available bits. This shortage of supply, relative to the demanded volume, is being caused by the physical characteristics of manufacturing HBM. HBM is silicon-intensive, and the trade ratio for HBM3 used to be 3:1, which means one wafer of HBM requires the capacity for three wafers of standard DDR memory.

As the industry moves to HBM4, HBM4E, and HBM5, this trade ratio gets even bigger. So, the aggressive ramp of HBM production consumes a disproportionate amount of total wafer capacity. This is reducing the overall supply of bits available for other markets like PC and smartphone memory.

Micron inventories are lean mainly in DRAM. To address this issue, Micron is trying to increase bit supply through node transitions. It is specifically doing the ramp of the 1-gamma node. However, as per the management, traditional node transitions are now less efficient than in previous eras. The bit growth hit by shrinking the die size is no longer adequate to match market demand. This reality pushes Micron to depend on greenfield fabrication facilities to hit necessary supply growth. The timeline for these greenfield projects lead to a lag in revenue realization.

The new fabrication facility in Idaho (Idaho 1) may not produce meaningful volume until H2/mid-27. The newly acquired Tongluo site in Taiwan may close in Q2-26, but it will not contribute to wafer output until H2-27. The new NAND fabrication facility in Singapore may not have first wafer outs until H2-28. The mega complex in New York is scheduled for production in 2030. Thus, in essence, it implies that for the year 2026 and the beginning of 2027, Micron has few mechanisms in place to improve bit output beyond the existing capacity limits.

Finally, to fund these expansions, Micron must incur heavy CapEx. The guidance for FY26 CapEx was set at $20 billion. The acquisition of the Tongluo site adds $1.8 billion to this outlay, plus associated tool installation costs. This massive outflow of cash hits hard on FCF. TTM levered FCF Margin of 1.05% is 90.24% below the sector median. There is high percentage growth in FCF, but the absolute margin remains thin relative to the sector as massive capital is needed to build and equip semiconductor cleanrooms.

Overall, the dependency on complex node transitions and new facility construction has execution risk. Any delay in the 1-gamma ramp and the equipping of the Tongluo facility may expand the supply shortage issue. As a result, I believe the delay may hurt MU stock price based on Street’s sentiment.

Takeaway

Micron finds itself in the sweet spot in the AI-driven memory boom, with HBM4 shipments already sold out in 2026, supply constraints, and increasing hyperscale demand. This gives Micron pricing power in the constrained supply environment, and Micron will benefit from high earnings growth in the next few years.

data.title: CTO Realty Growth: Shopping For Undervalued Shares Of This Growing REIT In Southeast
data.description:
Despite the elevated volatility in markets earlier this week amidst a major geopolitical conflict going on, one retail REIT stock actually is still up over +9% in the last month, Florida-based CTO Realty Growth (CTO).

This REIT owns and operates a portfolio of high-quality, retail-based properties located primarily in higher growth markets in the United States and also owns a meaningful interest in Alpine Income Property Trust.

Since I called it a buy in my Aug. 2025 article, it is up over +19%, reflecting the market confidence in this stock, and what I liked about it then was proven AFFO growth, portfolio growth, and high dividend yield.

It also caught my attention this week after sending out a press release that it is acquiring Palms Crossing in McAllen, Texas, which is already 98% leased.

In my follow-up article today, I'm exploring this stock again across 8 rating categories and in the wake of its recent earnings results on Feb. 19th.

Thesis Summary

After my updated research this time, I am reaffirming my prior buy rating on this stock, and agreeing with the latest buy consensus from SA analysts.

The worksheet below shows how my 8 factors contributed to the holistic score:

Today's thesis argues that despite some potential macro headwinds and recessionary clouds on the horizon, as well as some weaker margins than some peers, there are several key upside drivers for this stock and they include active/robust portfolio growth along with a focus on population growth regions like the southeast US, as well as positive operating cashflow supported by the huge real estate portfolio, technical patterns favoring bullishness, and a positive price upside forecast.

Macro & Sector Outlook

When it comes to this REIT, when thinking about the big picture and what sticks out, is that it is heavily focused on the southeast US, as we see in its portfolio, along with growth in New Mexico too.

So, what comes to mind from a macro level is population growth trends driving the demand for retail shopping experiences. For instance, last autumn, a forecast published by UT San Antonio said: "driven by migration trends and suburban sprawl, Texas population expected to hit 42.6M by 2060."

In addition, regional US media reported that estimates for 2025 are that "South Carolina added around 80,000 people, which was good for a growth rate of 1.5% — the fastest in the nation." Further, Florida Realtors reported that "Florida’s population surge is expected to continue through the end of the decade."

Besides the population tailwind in the south/southeast, we should consider 2026 consumer confidence and its impact on retail, since retail properties seem to be the core focus of this REIT.

For instance, to visualize some of their properties owned, this is the Pompano City Center which has tenants like TJ Maxx, Nordstrom, and others.

Aside from grocery and pharmacy, a lot of retail falls into the consumer discretionary niche, which can be recession-prone if consumer confidence and spending ability falls. Recent geopolitical developments in the Mideast can have a negative impact, with CNBC writing this week that it "risks a major oil supply disruption in the Middle East that, in a worst-case scenario, could trigger a global economic recession."

Prior to this event, I have cited several rather positive forecasts about the 2026 economy in recent articles, and notable is that Goldman Sachs(GS) also had a positive view in January, with their chief US economist saying "our strongest conviction views for 2026 are our above-consensus GDP growth forecast and our below-consensus inflation forecast."

Therefore, considering both that and the recent elevated risk if a regional war prolongs or expands geographically and driving energy costs through the roof, but also considering southeast US population growth trends as a key tailwind, I am modestly neutral/hold in this category of macroeconomic impact.

Competitive Positioning

In terms of competitive positioning, I compared CTO vs 3 similar REIT peers due to their focus on retail properties in the US are publicly-traded: Whitestone REIT (WSR), Frontview REIT (FVR), and Realty Income (O).

Based on peer data, with an ROE of just 1.7%, CTO falls behind in this peer group, and although its 5-year revenue CAGR of nearly +22% is respectable it is 3rd in this peer group.

In terms of market momentum, compared to the performance of a major benchmark like the S&P500 over the last decade, none of these stocks beat the benchmark, and with an impressive price return of +86% CTO actually beat a much bigger peer, Realty Income.

Besides these metrics, where CTO really could continue being competitive is their focus on population growth areas like the south/southeast US, and their portfolio diversification across many states in that region.

So, an optimistic neutral/hold score is fair in this section.

Company Growth Factors

Moving on, let's consider some company growth drivers and their impact.

We already mentioned the acquisition of the retail property in McAllen TX, which I think could drive upside through future rental income.

In addition, Q4 results (pg 1) reported that in Q4 the firm "acquired Pompano Citi Centre, a 509,000 square foot open-air retail center located in a submarket of Fort Lauderdale, Florida." I think this further grows the portfolio in the Florida region, another growth area.

Besides property acquisition, we should consider new leases signed as those drive future top-line revenue. Q4 results (pg 2) said "during the year ended December 31, 2025, the Company executed 87 new leases, renewals and extensions totaling 671,000 square feet."

Another key growth lever is a strategic investment in a new Whole Foods being developed in Atlanta, next to one of CTO's existing properties, as mentioned in the Q4 investor deck (pg 12), which I think can further drive this REIT's presence in more southeast states such as Georgia.

I would also point out that Seeking Alpha data has an expected fwd revenue growth for CTO of +11.2%, easily beating the sector average, and complementing the above growth trajectory of this REIT.

Given the overwhelming growth case above, I am highly bullish in this section.

Profitability Metrics

Next, I'll consider impacts to profitability, within the context of a REIT which is a unique type of business.

The EBITDA margin is a good metric in this case, as it strips out depreciation expense and accounting noise, and CTO had an impressive margin at +62.2% but was still beaten by two peers, Realty Income and Frontview:

Another key REIT metric, the FFO (funds from operations) saw muted results for CTO, with a 5 year FFO growth CAGR of -0.80%, far worse than the sector average, while the 5-year AFFO growth was slightly better at around +1.19%, but still muted vs the sector avg. By comparison, WSR had a 5 year FFO growth CAGR of +2.4%.

A ray of light comes from the analyst consensus, though, who anticipates a YoY FFO growth of around +7% this year and next too.

What's worth mentioning is that CTO's core strategy does not seem to be that of a triple net lease REIT, so I expect it to have higher property expenses and hence impacting earnings/profitability more, particularly if there is cost inflation in property expenses.

So, following NOI (net operating income) trends could point to how efficient this REIT is at managing its real estate. There are some positive signs there, as Q4 results (pg 2) said: "for the three months ended December 31, 2025, shopping center same property NOI increased by 4.3% versus the comparable 2024 period."

Thus, given the improving NOI, positive analyst consensus, but also some weaker margins and FFO growth metrics vs peers, I gave it a reasonable neutral/hold view in this section.

Balance Sheet Risk

Equity REITs like this have real estate as their major asset, an illiquid one, and we see from balance sheet data that CTO ended FY25 with nearly $1B in real estate.

Because the REIT depends on this portfolio to generate cashflow, which should more than cover debt payments and dividends, notable from Seeking Alpha data is that core operating cashflow grew YoY by +7.9%, which I think should add some confidence to investors that this REIT is a cashflow generating engine from its properties.

On the liability side, here is how their debt/equity compares vs peers:

CTO with a D/E of 1.14 is on the higher end of this peer group in terms of leverage, and you can see in the trend line that the D/E has grown over the last year, so I think this could move the risk needle up somewhat.

Another added element of risk is that there does not appear to be any recent ratings on CTO from the major agencies (Fitch, S&P, Moody's) so I won't be including that perspective here.

What I would say, though, is that investors should consider tenant over-concentration risk. In the case of CTO, though, according to the Q4 investor deck (pg 14), we see that none of its top 10 tenants individually contribute to more than 4% of ABR (annualized base rent) so this certainly lowers the risk profile, and of them is a consumer staples category, grocery chain Publix.

Given the low tenant concentration risk and geographic portfolio diversity across multiple states, as well as the positive operating cashflow, but also considering the elevated D/E and lack of major credit ratings, I would go with a very cautious bullish score in this section.

Dividend Outlook

I've consistently written in my REIT articles, and my REIT book, that dividend income is a major point of attraction towards REIT stocks, and so notable about CTO is its above-average fwd yield of +7.7%, which should get the attention of high-yield investors.

It also impressed me with a 10-year dividend CAGR of +49.8%, which I think shows its resilience over multiple market cycles and shocks, and I'd say for this sector the key one was the global pandemic and later the elevated inflation and tariffs scare.

As far as dividend safety, consider the quarterly dividend is $0.38, and income statement data shows the AFFO per share has ranges between $0.47 - $0.50 in the last several quarters, so that implies AFFO can cover the dividend, however the AFFO payout ratio may be on the higher side, above 75%, so that also leaves less cashflow buffer and can move the risk needle higher.

Given the high yield, proven growth through various cycles and shocks, and adequate AFFO coverage, but also considering the elevated payout ratio, I think a low bullish score is fair in this section.

Valuation Metrics

In this section, the focus is on my worksheet below which uses Seeking Alpha data and compares CTO's valuation vs peers, but also uses the fwd P/FFO multiple to determine a price forecast:

My price forecast implies further upside of +20% on this stock, and compared to the latest avg Wall St forecast of nearly +13%, both support a highly bullish view.

The stock also appears undervalued vs the peer average here. That could be reasonably justified, considering the lower EBITDA margin and FFO growth I mentioned earlier, and the portfolio consisting largely of retail property and some mortgage loans, so I think the market may not be wanting to pay such a premium on this portfolio right now.

The bull case, though, comes from the future potential of CTO. The analyst consensus already has a positive FFO outlook for the end of this year and next, for instance, and NOI has improved.

Technical Charting

Coming around to the close now, I'll wrap up by considering patterns in the following technical chart which compares the share price over the last year with the 50-day and 200-day SMA:

We can see a buy signal (golden cross formation) this winter when the 50-day crossed above the 200-day. The two moving averages remain parallel, but the short term one continues upward, and the share price also is still trading above it, indicating the 50-day SMA is still acting as support.

While some may argue that this can be a neutral argument (parallel moving averages) I am going with a bullish view here and the deciding factor is the 50-day SMA providing support to what appears to be continued bullish momentum.

This technical pattern only helps further move the overall argument towards a buy rating, which is what the overall score ended up being.

Risks and Challenges to Thesis

Since my view is bullish, and part of it is reliant on a growth thesis dependent on acquiring more retail properties, the limiting factor is the ability to develop new properties.

The current environment is one of high cost of capital (still elevated interest rates on CRE loans), construction cost inflation, limited and costly land to develop on, and competition from other larger REITs with capital to expand.

So, the challenge to my bullish call could come from those that argue this REIT has a limited growth runway beyond just acquiring some existing /already-built properties. The limiting factor is the cost and ability to develop new lands.

Conclusion

I've showed why I'm reaffirming my prior buy rating from 2025. Key strengths that remain are robust portfolio growth (via acquisition) in a population-growth region, NOI improvement, and dividend coverage, thereby overcoming some concerns including recession risk.

I also am including CTO in my dividend ideas for March, due to its high yield, coverage, and proven growth.

data.title: Broadcom: Blowout Earnings Add Even More Conviction
data.description:
Broadcom Inc. (AVGO) delivered blowout fiscal Q1 earnings yesterday after the market close, fortifying the long-term bullish case once again. The company's AI-related revenue continues soaring, while profitability is also expanding aggressively. The management's tone regarding AI-related business during the earnings call was very confident, which means that the company is likely to maintain its aggressive revenue and EPS for longer.

The stock spiked post-earnings by more than 6%, and I consider the market's reaction absolutely reasonable. Broadcom's share price didn't change much since my previous writeup, which means that valuation remains extremely attractive. I believe AVGO remains a Strong Buy.

FQ1 2026 earnings review

Broadcom's FQ1 2026 earnings were marked with another double beat. Both revenue and EPS were higher than consensus estimates, which was achieved for the fifth quarter in a row. Consolidated revenue growth accelerated to 29.5% on a year-over-year basis, which was the fourth quarter in a row with accelerating top line growth. Broadcom's non-GAAP EPS increased almost at the same rate as revenue did, increasing from $1.60 to $2.05. It was the record-breaking quarter for the company both in terms of revenue and EPS.

The company generated adjusted EBITDA of $13.1 billion, which yields a staggering 67.9% margin. It implies that operating leverage is still strong, and the management expects even a slight expansion to 68% in FQ2 according to the guidance. By the way, revenue guidance of $22 billion for the next quarter was almost 10% higher than Wall Street analysts expected. With that being said, Broadcom is expected to continue breaking its own revenue records further.

It is also vital to say that the forecasted FQ2 2026 revenue of $22 billion means that year-over-year growth will accelerate significantly in the next quarter. The company generated $15 billion in sales in FQ2 2025, which means we will likely see a staggering ~47% year-over-year revenue growth during the next earnings season.

Such an impressive revenue growth acceleration is explained by Broadcom's stellar ability to capitalize on AI infrastructure tailwinds. During the earnings call, the management cited a massive 106% year-over-year growth in AI-related sales. This impressive momentum in AI revenue growth will also accelerate in FQ2 as the management expects a 140% year-over year increase to $10.7 billion. Such a rapid growth in AI related revenue is very impressive considering the fact that Broadcom's consolidated quarterly sales surpassed $10 billion just two years ago, in FQ1 2024. And now, the AI business of the company alone generates approximately the same revenue as this entire giant did just eight quarters ago.

Such an aggressive forecast for AI-related revenue doesn't look unrealistic because of the fact that the fierce data center race between tech giants continues. All of the largest public players in generative AI (Amazon, Microsoft, Meta, and Google) confirmed during the Q4 2025 earnings season that they are ready to significantly boost investments in AI infrastructure during calendar 2026. Moreover, some of these companies also expressed opinions that investments in data center will continue growing in 2027 as well. Broadcom already boasts partnerships and contracts with all the major companies excelling in generative AI, meaning that it will certainly capitalize on this massive structural tailwind. During the earnings call, the management cited tougher and tougher competitive environment, but AVGO's $10+ billion annual R&D budget together with stellar record of success gives me confidence in its ability to protect its moat.

Finally, the bullish outlook is also fortified by the fact that Broadcom plans to continue its shareholder-friendly capital allocation with dividend increases and buybacks. During the call, the management announced that the Board of Directors has authorized additional share repurchase program worth $10 billion. This is another positive factor for investors as it reinforces the management's confidence in Broadcom's growth strategy which will anchor stability in cash generating capacity.

Valuation analysis

Broadcom's share price did not change much since my previous bullish writeup while there were multiple upward EPS revisions. It means that AVGO's forward P/E ratio setup has become even more attractive. The table below compares Broadcom's current forward P/E ratio setup to the one that was actual two months ago.

As we can see, AVGO's forward P/E ratios for FY2026-2028 compressed by double-digits, which is a substantial change. It means that upward earnings revisions for the next three fiscal years expanded by double-digits while share price remained flat. It is also vital to say that Broadcom's EPS is expected to significantly outpace the sector as its current PEG ratio of 0.95 is almost 30% lower compared to the sector.

Broadcom's PEG ratio estimate is based on consensus EPS forecasts, which doesn't guarantee success. However, Broadcom's flawless earnings beat record significantly increases the probability of actual results beating Wall Street forecasts.

Finally, the consensus target price of $455 is massively higher than AVGO's current share price even after the post-earnings rally. The most fascinating part is that even the lowest Wall Street target price estimate of $335 is approximately in line with the pre-market share price while I am writing this analysis.

Mitigating factors

The global geopolitical landscape is rapidly evolving in 2026 as the U.S. continues conducting its military operations. And if the military operation in Venezuela was quick and smooth, it is difficult to say the same about Iran because it is a large country with 90 million population and quite strong army. Moreover, the number of countries indirectly involved in this military operation is increasing as Iran continues expanding geography of its missile and drone launches. For example, just a few hours ago Iranian drones struck one of Azerbaijan's airports.

If this military operation continues longer-than-expected, it might adversely affect AVGO in two ways. First and foremost, investors are likely to become more fearful, which means a potential selloff in growth stocks. Second, it will disrupt global supply chains, which might affect operations of Broadcom or its key global partners. We see that oil prices have already spiked due to the military escalation, which is another adverse factor for the entire stock market because higher energy prices will push inflation up again.

Conclusion

AVGO's impressive FQ1 2026 earnings increase my conviction in bullish outlook for this stock. Moreover, the stock remains very attractively valued, which makes me reiterate the Strong Buy rating.

data.title: FSCO: High-Quality BDCs Have Spoiled Me, Hence I'm Still Avoiding This Pick
data.description:
In June 2025, I published my first article on FS Credit Opportunities Corp. (FSCO), which was a bullish one. The reason why I back then assigned a Buy rating was related mainly to conservative leverage and attractive floating vs. fixed-rate loan book positioning amid a declining interest rate environment.

However, a couple of months later, seeing that FSCO had significantly outperformed the Business Development Company ("BDC") index (BIZD) - by about 14% on a total return basis - and also recognizing that the Fund had an exposure to bankrupt First Brands business, I thought to myself that it was a timely moment to exit. The increasingly stretched dividend coverage was also what motivated me to assume a bearish stance, as outlined in the article FSCO: Big Downside Risk And Almost Certain Yield Cut (Downgrade). Finally, the presence of some similar yield but higher quality BDC alternatives made the choice even easier.

Since that moment, it has been "nothing but net" for my thesis:

Now, such kinds of drawdowns open the door for some logical questions around too aggressive discounting and thus potentially compelling entry points. These could be my initial thoughts from the bull's perspective:

  • FSCO yields 15.5%, which is the highest yield it ever has provided.
  • The Fund trades at ~27% discount to NAV.
  • The ~20% delta in total return performance relative to BDCs is interesting, potentially unjustified given the various overlaps between them.

However, in all fairness, I do not think that it is the right time to enter FSCO.

Let me flesh out the reasoning below.

Thesis review

First of all, I would like to establish an important context, which is that I am a structural private credit and/or BDC bull. Many of my closest followers will definitely agree with that. Also, my recent strategic piece on the BDC landscape, I think, clearly confirms that as well: Stop Panicking And Start Buying BDCs.

However, being a structural bull on a particular sector does not mean that everything that comes with a label that is associated with that particular sector is a buy. Actually, it is the opposite. The more advanced one becomes, the selectivity and taste tend to get more granular, nuanced, and, perhaps, spoiled (in a good sense).

So, with this being said, here is why FSCO is too risky for me and, also, from the P/NAV perspective, arguably, still too expensive.

There are 5 important aspects.

First, FSCO is not as cheap as it might seem. Yes, on an absolute level, ~27% discount is quite big, but in relative terms, the distance between FSCO valuation and BDC median valuation is not that wide. Namely, the median BDC trades at a 24% discount to NAV.

So, from this, I think we can put the argument of FSCO being relatively attractively valued aside and focus on the underlying fundamentals, which could theoretically support a greater valuation than for the median BDC.

Second, from the portfolio construction perspective, I would not give any points to FSCO, nor would I penalize it.

About 83% of the total portfolio is placed in senior secured first lien loans, which is the safest area to be from the lender's perspective. However, 83% in first lien means that 17% is somewhere else, where the risks are inherently higher. For example, we have second lien loans, unsecured debt, equity holdings, etc. In times like these, I would try to skew my allocations towards the safest possible area - i.e., senior secured first lien credit with at least 90% of the total exposure.

Third, and this is what I have indirectly implied from FSCO's portfolio in-place spread level, the underlying loans are much riskier than for most BDCs.

For example, the 2025 loan investments that FSCO made were underwritten at an average spread of SOFR + 661 bps. This kind of spread is what we don't see among high-quality BDC names and even not in the LMM private credit area, where the compensation for risk tends to be higher. The average spread that we can basically see across the board in the BDC space is between 475 bps and 575 bps over SOFR. Anything that is above is usually associated with a second lien, PIK or ARR loan.

CION Investment Corporation (CION) is a good example, which has a huge concentration of PIK and thus closer spread levels to FSCO than say for top-tier names such as Blackstone Secured Lending Fund (BXSL) and Kayne Anderson BDC, Inc. (KBDC). But this is also why CION trades at a P/NAV of 0.56x. So, as we know, the higher the risk, the higher the spreads.

Fourth, the earnings quality confirms the previous point. FSCO has 21.7% of total income coming in the form of PIK. It is a very, very high number. Much higher than the BDC sector average of ~7.5%.

There are three BDCs that have their PIK figures in 20%+ territory: TriplePoint Venture Growth (TPVG), Horizon Technology Finance (HRZN) and CION. And this is how their share prices have evolved since 2024:

It looks like FSCO has some catching up to do.

Fifth, FSCO has a deeply undercovered dividend. In Q4 2025, FSCO generated NII per share of $0.12, while the total distributions that were paid during that quarter amounted to slightly above $0.20 per share.

The bottom line

Even though I think that the fears about private credit exploding are way overblown and the corresponding BDC (private credit) discounts have become extremely compelling, it does not mean that everything in this complex is a buy.

We have to respect the market and understand that, while it is indeed emotional, there is usually some valid ground behind its actions or reactions to incoming data. In other words, it is time to buy BDCs, but with caution and a defensive bias.

In this respect, FSCO is anything but defensive. The combination of massive PIK, meaningful concentration outside of senior secured first lien credit, and aggressive spreads indicates that the risk level here is higher than what we can get in the BDC space, where the average discounts are rather similar to that of FSCO.

I am still avoiding FSCO after the Q4 2025 report.
